key_insight: Adaptive superpixels compress large 2D diffraction datasets by merging low-information pixels while preserving key structure, enabling faster and more interpretable downstream analysis.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical preprocessing step for 4D-STEM and synchrotron diffraction workflows where data volume is a major bottleneck for machine learning and phase mapping.
method: Group pixels into variable-area superpixels based on variance minimization.
Compare top-down K-means clustering with bottom-up agglomerative clustering.
Accelerate superpixel construction using Gaussian random projection before distance-based clustering.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports superior scaling and interpretability for the bottom-up method and over 100-fold acceleration for phase mapping with non-negative matrix factorization on a 4D-STEM dataset.
watch_item: Because the approach is distance-based and data-dependent, superpixel quality may be sensitive to parameter choices and the choice of projection/clustering settings.
paper_title: Using superpixels for interpretable feature reduction in large 2D diffraction datasets
